text: The camera view focuses on the right lower quadrant, where adhesions to the appendix are visible.
The surgeon divides adhesions from the omentum to gain access to the appendix in the right lower quadrant.
The laparoscopic view reveals the appendix, the terminal ileum, and surrounding adhesions in the right lower quadrant.
The surgeon uses the Ligasure energy device to transect the adhesions, allowing for a clearer assessment of the appendix and surrounding anatomy in the right lower quadrant.
The surgeon visually identifies the ligament of Treves, the mesoappendix, and notes that the base of the appendix is positioned posterior to these structures.
The surgeon considers transecting the ligament of Traves to expose the base of the appendix, which is located posterior to the mesoappendix.
The surgeon cuts the ligament of Traves, revealing the base of the appendix, which becomes clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon transects the mesoappendix, severing the blood vessels that supply the appendix, with careful attention to surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses an energy device to transect the mesoappendix, ensuring hemostasis while protecting surrounding structures such as the cecum and terminal ileum.
The base of the appendix is exposed, prepared for transection, with surrounding structures such as the cecum and terminal ileum visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a laparoscopic vascular stapler to close and transect the appendix at its base, ensuring the ileocecal valve is protected and away from the stapler before firing.
The surgeon irrigates and aspirates the abdominal cavity, confirming hemostasis and verifying the integrity of the staple line at the base of the appendix.
The surgeon ensures that the ileocecal valve is positioned away from the surgical field, maintaining a clear area for the ongoing appendectomy procedure.
The surgeon places the transected appendix into an endocatch bag for removal from the abdominal cavity.
